item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually really enjoyed this one.

I've just been accepted onto the foundation so i'm still relatively fresh faced about analysing articles. That being said, I enjoyed the fact that the piece is brief and focuses more on the ethical dilemma of the research, rather than trying to be a needlessly grandiose piece.

The feedback from other readers is definitely insightful and should be taken with more weight than my own. However, I find the sudden nature of the addendum somewhat interesting. Some testing logs building up to the incident report, showing the reactions from foundation personnel would definitely contribute to the overall weight of bates' actions.

I hope you can discern some value from my thoughts :)

item.title: ...eh.
item.content:encoded:
I'm gonna have to agree with… just about everyone else.

The article needs more build-up to the final addendum; maybe some test logs, with notes from Bates showing his gradual disgust towards the Foundation and Kijuro in particular, what exactly the tests involved, and maybe hint at what happens to the victims/test subjects besides just being thrown into some drugstore-variety Fire And Brimstone HellTM.

Overall, no vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nthing the underdeveloped comments but this definitely has the potential to be a nice short n sweet article if the feedback given here is taken.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I thought I'd taken care of this before, but I guess I didn't. I've removed the tags, so a member of the tag team can properly tag the page. If you're unfamiliar with our tagging system, we ask that you don't tag your own pages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvote.

(+) Nice horror about the voices and engulfed flames.

(-) MEMES, fuck that with a long stick.
(-) The final addendum is typical 'scp is horrible so he deserved it'.
(-) No exploration or such, just a mention of a cliched hell.
(-) Underdeveloped: The relationship between the two, what actually happens to the victims, origin of the memetic, etc.

I could see this worked with a few adjustments, and more originality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree entirely with Mann, who more eloquently summed up the issues with the article that I could.

I can see what is trying to be done, but it comes out of left field rather than being built up to. Too on-the-nose.

And it's a bit of a shame because the role of a guard is almost akin to the role of a D-Class in most articles - they're just passengers, they don't make the big decisions, and often they don't get much character - so the perspective of a guard who has to sit and watch all of this terrible stuff happening and how they react to that is a really good idea.

No vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. I like the concept, but I'm not feeling the execution.

I feel like Calamity's hit the main issue. The main core is the conflict between Kijuro and Bates, and there just isn't any real set-up for it. We're tossed right into it without having any connection to these people.

There are two things I'd like to see expanded here. First, more examples of the effects to help sell the idea that this is sending people to actual, literal hell. The multiple voices screaming is a good start, but some more details like that could make it really pop. Maybe glimpses in the flames of previous subjects, or voices actually pleading for help rather than just screams.

Second, some more set-up of the conflict. Give us some notes and addenda to show the growing tension between the two men. Make us believe that Bates would just go to the extreme of forcing Kijuro to taste his own medicine (as opposed to, say, just shooting him). Attempts by Bates to appeal to higher authorities, attempts to reason with Kijuro. Maybe show Bates's realization that Kijuro is going outside of normal testing parameters (or the realization that those are normal parameters).

I feel like you have the potential for a good story here, but you haven't told it yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the base idea is cute, if a tad boring. But it's poorly presented, and I don't like the story that this is trying to tell.

I don't care about D-Class personnel, nor do I really care about the ethics of using D-Class personnel to test anomalies, so when I see documents like this that try to throw a light on this ethical issue in some way, it bounces right off of me.

In this case, the entire meat of the article beyond the cute-but-boring effect, is a guy that gets mad because D-Class personnel are being killed and sent to hell, so instead of making the allegations against the doc first and taking his concerns to a Site Director, some other superior, or appeals to the Ethics Committee or some other thing that a sensible, professional human being employed by the Foundation would do, this self-righteous vigilante hero just… murders a head researcher, makes a long and painfully presumptuous spiel ("if that video actually does what it says it does" is not the strongest case to try to make when justifying cold-blooded murder) about how there are limits to what is and isn't 'appropriate' torture/death sentences for D-Class personnel, and then he dies.

There's no story here for me. -1, unfortunately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And possibly end up being taken to hell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
might be just me but the idea is pretty bland. you laugh you erupt in flames.
..?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great take on those “you laugh you lose” clickbait videos. Some nitpicks like how the word laugh shouldn’t be in italics in the 4th paragraph, and in the incident addendum, the line “SCP involved” should be removed considering that the incident report itself is in the SCP-3384 article.
+1

item.title: Author
item.content:encoded: Well. It's been years on this site, and it's been a long time since I really gave a good attempt at writing for it. Here it is. Hope you all like it.
